project_overview.project_name: Digital CASA (Central Asia South Asia) Project - Afghanistan
project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase access to more affordable Internet, crowd-in private investment in the ICT sector, and improve the Government's capacity to deliver digital Government services in Afghanistan, by contributing to the development of a regionally integrated digital infrastructure and enabling environment.
project_overview.total_financing: SDR 35.1 million (US$51 million equivalent) IDA Grant
project_overview.beneficiaries: Citizens, businesses, government institutions in Afghanistan; spillover effects in other landlocked countries in Central Asia and South Asia. Direct beneficiaries include private sector telecoms companies, IT companies, digital entrepreneurs, and government agencies.

primary_locations: Provincial capitals (25 of 34 provinces already connected to OFC)
Cross-border connections with China, Iran, Pakistan, Russia
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - Afghanistan is classified as fragile and conflict-affected. Between January-September 2017, 2,640 people were killed and 5,380 injured. Over 200,000 internally displaced in first half of 2017. Rising insecurity affecting business confidence and economic activity.

key_components.description: Crowd-in private sector investments to improve regional and domestic fiber-optic connectivity. Includes: (1.1) Pre-purchase of international bandwidth and viability gap funding for optical fiber in unviable areas; (1.2) Government Network (GovNet) providing broadband to public institutions; (1.3) Internet Exchange Points (IXP) for faster traffic exchange.
key_components.financing: $30 million ($14 million regional IDA, $16 million national IDA)

key_components.description: Establish critical e-Government foundations and deliver digital services. Includes: (2.1) Shared e-Government infrastructure (Government Cloud, Disaster Recovery Data Center, common e-service enablers); (2.2) Enhanced national portal and four priority e-services (e-visa, public grievances gateway, e-licensing, e-catalogue).

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Enabling Environment (Policy and Regulatory Frameworks)
key_components.description: Technical assistance to MCIT and ATRA for policy, legal, and regulatory modifications. Includes development of national broadband policy, right-of-way guidelines, interconnection regulations, quality of service standards, tariff regulations. Focus on implementing Open Access Policy and harmonizing regional frameworks.
key_components.financing: $3 million ($2 million regional IDA, $1 million national IDA)

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Project Management and Institutional Strengthening
key_components.description: Project management, coordination, procurement, financial management, M&E, safeguards implementation. Institutional strengthening including training for leaders, government officials, technical staff, and ATRA staff. Emphasis on training women (target 30% female participation).
key_components.financing: $8 million ($4 million regional IDA, $4 million national IDA)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) as borrower and lead implementing agency, in cooperation with Afghanistan Telecom Regulatory Authority (ATRA) and other beneficiary line ministries (Ministry of Economy, Ministry of Public Works, Ministry of Energy, Ministry of Railways, Ministry of Education).
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Steering Committee led by Ministry of Finance providing strategic guidance and oversight. Digital CASA Implementation Unit (DCIU) housed within MCIT, headed by Project Director reporting to Minister. Regional coordination through government-to-government engagement among participating countries (Afghanistan, Kyrgyz Republic, and potential future participants). Discussions ongoing regarding regional anchor institution (Central Asia Regional Economic Cooperation, Central Asia Research and Education Network, universities, etc.).
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for Borrowers (July 1, 2016) apply. International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for large contracts (goods/works >$5 million, services >$200,000). National Competitive Bidding for smaller contracts. Prior review threshold (PRT) applied for high-risk contracts. MCIT Procurement Directorate responsible for procurement; NPA provides facilitation. Procurement risk rated High, mitigated by hiring international procurement specialist and dedicated national staff. Standard Bidding Documents used. Emphasis on transparency, publication on NPA website, UNDB, and national newspapers.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: MCIT responsible for M&E with biannual progress reports. Midterm review at end of year 3. Standard formats and guidelines for data collection. Coordination with telecommunications operators and stakeholders. Beneficiary feedback mechanisms including annual satisfaction surveys (target 80% satisfaction). E-Government customer perception surveys. Grievance redress mechanism with tracking of complaints. Results framework includes PDO-level indicators, intermediate outcome indicators, and citizen engagement indicators.

identified_risks.risk_description: Political volatility and rising levels of insecurity in the country that could increase costs and delay infrastructure rollout. Possible change in governments could lead to weak regional coordination or withdrawal from Digital CASA.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular security assessments by MCIT and World Bank team. Implementation schedules and financing allocation adjusted accordingly. Advanced preparation of bidding documents under PPG to mitigate slow implementation. Close policy dialogue with governments to ensure broad consensus. Regular monitoring of political developments.

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall macroeconomic context affecting ICT sector. Economic growth projected at 2.6% (2017), 3.2% (2018), 3.6% (2020) - significantly below 9.6% average (2003-2012). Poverty rate 39.1%, unemployment 22.6%. Weak labor demand and security constraints.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ICT sector has been relatively resilient to macroeconomic risks historically. Project design expected to reinforce this resilience. Project fully funded by IDA Grant, limiting exposure to macroeconomic volatility.

identified_risks.risk_description: Possible delays in introducing sector reforms promoting competition in international and domestic Internet connectivity. Regulatory framework implementation may be slow.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank and IFC teams providing advisory services to ATRA. $5 million PPG financing advisers to ensure ATRA capacity for issuing downstream regulations early in implementation. Specific disbursement conditions for Subcomponent 1.1 to ensure open access principles. Significant technical assistance under project for regulatory capacity building.

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall complexity of project design including both connectivity and e-Government components. Risk of implementation delays and coordination challenges.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Phased approach to reduce complexity. Application of existing working models. Full stakeholder involvement. Regional partnerships and communications. Systematic review of project design to limit complex activities. Minimization of procurement packages where possible.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Government's capacity to implement and govern project requiring significant interministerial coordination. Limited experience with complex World Bank projects in some agencies. Weak institutional capacity in government institutions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: MCIT has significant experience implementing World Bank projects. Substantial support for capacity building following international good practices. Minimization of procurement packages. Close World Bank supervision. Hiring of competent implementation staff. Training programs for government officials at all levels.

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited capacity of MCIT staff to manage fiduciary aspects of project. Financial management and procurement risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports hiring and training of required staff. Internal audit consultant (local) hired under project reporting to MCIT Head of Internal Audit. Semiannual internal audit reports submitted to World Bank. World Bank training on fiduciary requirements. Periodic assessment of fiduciary performance. FM Manual revised and approved by World Bank.

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertainties regarding level of private sector investments for wholesale optical fiber networks. Limited private sector interest or capacity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: PPG financing technical feasibility study identifying viability gaps in close coordination with private operators. Continuous consultations with private sector throughout implementation. World Bank advising on bidding process with adjustments as needed. Implementation of regulatory mechanisms through consensus building. Engagement and sensitization of key government stakeholders (Ministry of Economy, Ministry of Public Works, etc.).

identified_risks.risk_description: Physical infrastructure activities (OFC excavation) may cause temporary impacts including partial loss of productive assets, land requirements, temporary access limitations. Involuntary resettlement risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed. Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared and disclosed. MCIT recruiting full-time Safeguards Specialist. Safeguard and GRM training for project staff and contractors. Recording and reporting mechanisms for land requirements and grievance handling. Site-specific ESMP for subprojects.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Category B - Partial Assessment. Physical work under Component 1 may cause adverse environmental and social impacts including occupational health and safety issues. Risks generally low as project uses existing right-of-way along roads and power transmission networks for underground fiber-optic cable burial. No tower financing foreseen.
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Functional GRM established. Project-level grievance redress mechanism with proper recording and reporting. Annual beneficiary feedback mechanisms through surveys. E-Government customer perception surveys. Grievance tracking and monitoring. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for project-affected communities. World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding WB non-compliance with policies.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender gap in Internet access (81% of users are male). Only 19% of women in labor force vs 87% of men. Afghanistan ranks 154 of 188 on UN Gender Inequality Index. Project targets: (1) 30% of MCIT and ATRA employees trained to be female, focusing on IT and IT-related disciplines; (2) Bidding documents for e-services development include provisions to train female staff for long-term maintenance and management; (3) Emphasis on training women at all levels of capacity building; (4) Focus on increasing opportunities for women in digital entrepreneurship and skills development.
vulnerable_groups: Rural populations (only 2% Internet usage vs 16% in Kabul)
Poor and low-income citizens (retail Internet price $37/Mbps/month unaffordable)
Women (significant gender gap in Internet access and labor force participation)
Youth (400,000 Afghans entering labor market annually)
Internally displaced persons (200,000+ displaced in first half of 2017)
Returnee refugees (296,000+ returned from Iran and Pakistan)
Low-skilled and illiterate workers (particularly high unemployment)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. Addresses fragility through: (1) Digital infrastructure as enabler of transparency and accountability to combat institutionalized corruption; (2) E-Government services improving government efficiency and service delivery; (3) Regional integration promoting cooperation and reducing isolation; (4) Economic diversification and job creation for vulnerable populations; (5) Improved connectivity supporting early warning systems for climate and disaster impacts. Project recognizes ICT as vital tool for managing critical transition from security/development dominated by international community to government-led approach. Alignment with Afghanistan National Peace and Development Framework 2017-2021 which recognizes ICT as enabler of social and economic development.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan classified as fragile and conflict-affected state. Key fragility factors: (1) Rising insecurity - 2,640 killed and 5,380 injured Jan-Sept 2017; (2) Rapid internal displacement - 200,000+ displaced in first half 2017; (3) Refugee returns - 296,000+ from Iran and Pakistan; (4) Economic stagnation - growth 2.2-2.6% vs 9.6% historical average; (5) High poverty - 39.1% in 2013/14, expected to remain high; (6) High unemployment - 22.6%, especially among women and low-skilled; (7) Weak institutional capacity and governance; (8) Limited access to services, particularly in rural areas; (9) Vulnerability to climate shocks and natural disasters (ranked 11th most vulnerable globally); (10) Dependence on aid flows for growth.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation assessed regularly by MCIT and World Bank team with implementation schedules adjusted accordingly. Rising insecurity could increase component costs and delay infrastructure rollout. Physical infrastructure activities (fiber-optic cable installation) may face security challenges in conflict-affected areas. Project design includes provisions for implementation in phases and flexibility to adjust based on security context. Advanced preparation of bidding documents under PPG to mitigate risks of slow implementation due to security delays. Regional connectivity via China provides alternative routes improving resilience. Underground fiber-optic infrastructure more resilient to security-related damage than aerial cables.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Significant displacement and refugee issues. Over 200,000 internally displaced persons in first half of 2017 due to conflict. Over 296,000 refugees returned from Iran and Pakistan. Project addresses through: (1) Improved connectivity and digital services supporting service delivery to displaced and vulnerable populations; (2) E-Government services accessible via mobile devices reducing need for physical travel; (3) Digital job opportunities and economic diversification; (4) Resettlement Policy Framework addressing potential impacts of infrastructure activities on affected populations; (5) Grievance redress mechanisms for project-affected communities; (6) Safeguards implementation including site-specific ESMPs for subprojects.
